title: TPU v1 · Software Stack: TensorFlow → XLA → CISC Commands → MXU
oneLine: The programming interface decides who keeps the array full. Users do not write assembly; XLA compiles TensorFlow graphs into roughly a dozen CISC commands that the host sends over PCIe. Hardware executes them, while the TensorFlow/XLA ecosystem provides the durable advantage.

legend.name: ① TensorFlow model
legend.desc: The user entry point is a high-level dataflow graph describing what to compute. Users never handle the systolic array, Unified Buffer capacity, or instruction encoding; the compiler hides hardware complexity.

legend.name: ② XLA compiler
legend.desc: The center of the stack: fuse operators, lay out tensors, choose what resides in the 24 MiB Unified Buffer, and lower the graph to a short CISC stream. Compiler-managed movement is the necessary partner to a software-managed UB.

legend.name: ③ CISC instruction set
legend.desc: TPU exposes roughly a dozen coarse commands such as Read_Weights, MatrixMultiply, and Activate. Each performs substantial work, matching host-driven deterministic batch inference rather than fine-grained CPU-style execution.

legend.name: MatrixMultiply command
legend.desc: One command drives the entire 256×256 systolic array through a large matrix multiply. Fetch and decode cost is amortized across tens of thousands of MAC operations, enabling minimal control hardware.

legend.name: ④ Hardware execution
legend.desc: The MXU and Unified Buffer execute submitted commands without making scheduling decisions. Intelligence resides in the compiler; brute-force arithmetic resides in the array—the boundary that defines the programming model.

steps.cap: ① Users build a TensorFlow dataflow graph that describes what to compute without exposing hardware details.
② XLA fuses operators, lays out tensors, and decides how values occupy the 24 MiB Unified Buffer.
③ Compilation produces roughly a dozen CISC commands, including Read_Weights, MatrixMultiply, and Activate.
④ The host sends commands over PCIe; one MatrixMultiply command drives the entire 256×256 array.
⑤ The full stack combines framework usability at the top, array throughput at the bottom, and compiler translation between them.

before: Choose the programmer: The accelerator serves ML engineers who write models, not hardware engineers who write assembly. Users should keep their familiar framework, which requires a compiler layer rather than a new exposed ISA.
Decisions to make: RISC versus coarse CISC, instruction count and granularity, host-driven versus autonomous execution, framework/compiler integration, and the PCIe control plane.

Required inputs: The regular, matrix-heavy batch-inference workload (§3.1) and a key hardware constraint—the 24 MiB Unified Buffer is a software-managed scratchpad without hardware caching, so software must schedule movement explicitly.
Hard constraint: Ecosystem is a moat. Even the fastest array is unusable without the TensorFlow/XLA toolchain, so success depends equally on hardware and reuse of the existing framework.
during: Move complexity into the compiler: Hardware removes caches, out-of-order execution, and branch prediction (§3.9). XLA replaces that intelligence at compile time through operator fusion, tensor layout, and UB-residency planning.

Choose CISC over RISC: A deliberately coarse MatrixMultiply command occupies the whole 256×256 array. Tens of thousands of MACs amortize fetch/decode overhead, minimizing control logic and fitting deterministic batch inference.

Treat a small ISA as a feature: About a dozen commands—Read_Weights, MatrixMultiply, Activate, and others—map to coarse neural-network primitives. Fewer commands mean simpler decode and pipelines, preserving area for MACs.

Use a host-driven model: TPU is a coprocessor, not an autonomous CPU. The host pushes compiled commands over PCIe 3.0, eliminating a complex on-chip fetch/scheduling frontend while requiring host software to order the sequence.

Keep the boundary clear: Scheduling, layout, and fusion belong to the compiler; matrix arithmetic belongs to the array. Because software fully manages the UB, hardware can omit cache coherence and remain much simpler.

Accept the cost: Static compile-time layout makes runtime simple but handles dynamic or irregular shapes poorly. Tight TensorFlow/XLA coupling also means other frameworks need a dedicated compiler backend.

result.label: Programming entry
result.value: TensorFlow dataflow graph; users avoid hardware details

result.label: ISA style
result.value: Coarse-grained CISC; roughly a dozen commands

result.label: Ecosystem advantage
result.value: Reuse TensorFlow/XLA and reduce migration cost

after: Verify the end-to-end compiler path: Run real models through TensorFlow → XLA → CISC → array, checking correctness and utilization. Improve compiler layout and fusion before changing hardware when utilization is low.

Reconcile memory and dataflow: If XLA layouts do not fit the UB or frequently spill to DDR3, revisit the 24 MiB memory capacity and dataflow assumptions. The programming model and memory hierarchy are coupled.
Propagate constraints: Give microarchitecture (④) final ISA encodings and timing; give host software the PCIe submission protocol and queue model so the host-driven path does not bottleneck.

Invest in the ecosystem: Maintain the XLA TPU backend, operator libraries, and documentation so more TensorFlow models run unchanged. Long-term interface value comes from sustained software engineering, not a one-time hardware release.
source: In-Datacenter Performance Analysis of a TPU, Jouppi et al., ISCA 2017. Facts disclosed in the paper are distinguished from illustrative examples and standard industry practice; the latter are not presented as disclosed TPU v1 implementation details.
